comments:
Summary:
The paper presents an engineering approach to decomposing scanned Bangla documents (newspaper pages) into regions such as headlines, sub-headlines, columns and images, and to perform deskewing and autorotation. The approach uses Canny edge detection, projection-like analysis to group pixels into black/white regions, geometric and pixel-ratio based filters to detect images, and script-specific cues (line height, matra line statistics) to classify text regions and decide rotation. The authors report experiments on ~300 pages (decomposition) and 70 skewed/rotated images (deskew/rotation), with reported high accuracy for deskewing and reasonable rotation success. 

Strengths:
- Addresses a useful practical problem for Bangla OCR preprocessing (page segmentation and orientation correction). The use of matra-line statistics for rotation is appropriate for Bangla script and is a reasonable language-specific cue.
- System appears to work on real scanned newspaper pages and the authors report experiments on several hundred pages.

Major weaknesses / concerns:
1) Lack of methodological detail and clarity: key algorithmic steps are described qualitatively but important details are missing. For example, how exactly are the "horizontal and vertical area of every pixel" computed, what are the threshold values and how were they chosen, how is skew angle estimated (algorithm/formula), and how are the filters' thresholds set. There is no pseudocode or formal algorithm description.
2) Weak and incomplete evaluation: the paper gives only a few summary numbers (e.g. "accuracy of our de-skew system is approximately 100%" for 70 images; rotation 97.83%), but no definition of accuracy, no confusion matrices, and little quantitative breakdown for segmentation (precision/recall numbers are mentioned as given below but are not present in the provided text). There is no dataset description (source, resolution, noise levels), no ground truth annotation process, and no statistical analysis.
3) Poor comparison with prior work: related work is cited, but the paper does not compare its performance against existing systems (including the Bangla-specific work it cites) on shared datasets or benchmarks. This makes it impossible to judge whether the contribution is a substantive improvement.
4) Limited novelty: methods used are largely standard (edge detection, projection/profile analysis, geometric heuristics). The matra-based rotation cue is language-aware but not discussed deeply. The contribution is mainly an application and integration of common techniques with ad-hoc thresholds rather than a new algorithmic advance.
5) Presentation and writing quality: the manuscript contains multiple typographical and formatting errors, figures are referenced but not clearly presented in the text excerpt, and many passages are repetitive. Important tables/figures with quantitative results appear missing. This impairs reproducibility and readability.
6) Missing handling of tables and other page artifacts: the authors acknowledge tables are treated as images; no solution is provided. Robustness to noise, varying fonts, multi-column layouts with embedded images, and other realistic newspaper complexities are not explored.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Provide a clear, step-by-step algorithmic description (pseudocode) and precise definitions of all computed features (how line-height, matra index, pixel ratios, skew angle, etc. are computed).
- Report thorough quantitative experiments: define metrics (precision, recall, F1) for each class (headline, sub-headline, column, image), include per-class confusion matrices, and give dataset details (number of pages, resolution, source, train/test split). Show representative success and failure cases.
- Compare against prior methods (at least the Bangla work cited) on comparable data or discuss why direct comparison is not possible.
- Analyze parameter sensitivity and robustness to noise, image quality, and layout variation. Provide ablation experiments (e.g., effect of matra-based rotation vs. generic rotation methods).
- Improve writing, fix formatting/typos, and include figures/tables with captions and clear references.
- Release dataset and code if possible to aid reproducibility.

Overall recommendation:
The paper describes a practical system with some language-specific ideas but lacks sufficient technical detail, rigorous evaluation, and comparisons to be convincing as a research contribution in its current form. With substantial revisions (clarification, expanded experiments, better presentation) it could be a useful engineering contribution for Bangla OCR preprocessing.
